1:
On 1 Timothy 4:1 — “Now the Spirit speaketh expressly, that in the latter times some shall depart from the faith, giving heed to seducing spirits, and doctrines of devils” — the witnesses mark the prophecy of apostasy.

The Eastern fathers note the contrast. Chrysostom: those who fall from the faith, after wanderings, are borne “into the very gulf of perdition.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “expressly” (rhetos), in plain words; the Spirit’s warning. Ellicott: a strong contrast (de) to the mystery of godliness. Wesley: “the latter times” extend from the ascension to the judgment.

The verse foretells a coming apostasy. The Spirit plainly declares that in later times some will abandon the faith, following deceiving spirits and demonic teachings. Defection is prophesied.

So the witnesses see Paul, against the bright confession of the mystery of godliness, set the dark prophecy of apostasy. The witnesses note the source and certainty: “the Spirit speaketh expressly” — the Holy Spirit foretells this plainly and in express words, whether through Old Testament prophecy, the words of Christ, or Christian prophets; it is no mere conjecture but sure revelation. They mark the time: “in the latter times” — the gospel age, reaching (as Wesley says) from Christ’s ascension to his coming, in which such defection would appear. They observe the defection: “some shall depart from the faith” — apostatize, fall away from the truth once held; and its cause: “giving heed to seducing spirits, and doctrines of devils” — heeding deceiving spirits and teachings that have their origin in demons, error inspired from below. They note the sharp contrast Ellicott marks: just after the glorious mystery of Christ, Paul warns that some will forsake it for satanic error. They observe Chrysostom’s image of the apostate, having let go the anchor of faith, borne at last into the gulf of perdition. The verse thus opens the warning against false teachers: the Spirit has foretold a falling-away in which men, seduced by demonic doctrine, abandon the faith — the specific marks of which Paul now describes.

So the witnesses see Paul, against the bright confession of the mystery of godliness, set the dark prophecy of apostasy. The source and certainty: “the Spirit speaketh expressly” — the Holy Spirit foretells this plainly, no mere conjecture but sure revelation. The time: “in the latter times” — the gospel age, from Christ’s ascension to his coming (Wesley). The defection: “some shall depart from the faith” — apostatize from the truth; its cause: “giving heed to seducing spirits, and doctrines of devils” — error inspired from below. Chrysostom’s image: the apostate, having let go the anchor of faith, is borne at last into the gulf of perdition.
2:
On 1 Timothy 4:2 — “Speaking lies in hypocrisy; having their conscience seared with a hot iron” — the witnesses mark the character of the false teachers.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the channel. Robertson: “through the hypocrisy of men that speak lies” (en hypokrisei pseudologon); “branded in their own conscience as with a hot iron” (kekausteriasmenon). Ellicott: through the hypocrisy of liars. Wesley: “consciences as senseless and unfeeling as flesh seared with a hot iron.”

The verse exposes the agents of the apostasy. It comes through the hypocrisy of liars whose consciences are seared. Deceit and a deadened conscience mark them.

So the witnesses see Paul describe the human instruments through whom the demonic error spreads. The witnesses note the channel: the apostasy comes “through the hypocrisy of men that speak lies” — deceivers who, under a mask of piety, teach falsehood as truth; their lying is wrapped in hypocrisy, a pretended holiness and zeal that lends their errors credit. They mark the dreadful inward state: “having their conscience seared with a hot iron” — the figure of cauterized flesh, branded and deadened; their conscience, once sensitive to right and wrong, has been burned past feeling, so that they can teach lies and practice deceit without remorse. They observe Wesley’s point that such a conscience is as senseless and unfeeling as flesh seared with a hot iron — the moral sense destroyed. They note that this twofold mark — hypocritical deceit without and a deadened conscience within — explains how men can propagate soul-destroying error with the appearance of sanctity. They observe that the false asceticism Paul will name was precisely such hypocritical lying, paraded as superior holiness. The verse thus unmasks the teachers of the apostasy: hypocritical liars whose seared consciences feel no shame — men who, having lost moral feeling, spread demonic doctrine under a show of piety, beginning with the prohibitions named next.

So the witnesses see Paul describe the human instruments through whom the demonic error spreads. The channel: the apostasy comes “through the hypocrisy of men that speak lies” — deceivers who, under a mask of piety, teach falsehood as truth; their lying wrapped in a pretended holiness that lends their errors credit. The dreadful inward state: “having their conscience seared with a hot iron” — cauterized, branded and deadened past feeling, so that they teach lies without remorse. Wesley’s point: such a conscience is as senseless as flesh seared with a hot iron — the moral sense destroyed.
3:
On 1 Timothy 4:3 — “Forbidding to marry, and commanding to abstain from meats, which God hath created to be received with thanksgiving of them which believe and know the truth” — the witnesses mark the false asceticism.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the errors. Robertson: “forbidding to marry” (kolyonton gamein); cf. the Gnostic asceticism Paul condemns (Colossians 2:21); the Essenes forbade marriage. Adam Clarke applies it to the Romanists forbidding priests to marry. Ellicott: a perilous delusion growing into Gnostic dogma. Wesley names the prohibitions of meats and marriage.

The verse names the marks of the apostasy. It forbids marriage and commands abstinence from foods that God created to be received with thanksgiving. A false asceticism is its symptom.

So the witnesses see Paul identify the apostasy by its ascetic prohibitions. The witnesses note the two marks: “forbidding to marry” — condemning marriage as unholy, an error of those (the Essenes, certain Gnostics, and, as Clarke applies it, later sects forbidding clergy to wed) who counted celibacy a higher sanctity; and “commanding to abstain from meats” — imposing as religious duty the avoidance of certain foods, as if these defiled. They mark the refutation Paul attaches: these things — both marriage and food — God “created to be received with thanksgiving”; they are God’s good gifts, meant to be used with gratitude, not shunned as evil. They observe that the false teachers thus slander the Creator, calling unclean what he made good and gave for use. They note Ellicott’s point that this was a perilous delusion that would grow into full Gnostic dogma, despising the body and the created order. They observe the qualification: the gifts are received rightly by “them which believe and know the truth” — believers, who understand that all God’s creatures are clean. The verse thus exposes the apostasy’s symptom — a false, world-denying asceticism forbidding marriage and food — and answers it by the goodness of God’s creation, the principle the next verses press.

So the witnesses see Paul identify the apostasy by its ascetic prohibitions. The two marks: “forbidding to marry” — condemning marriage as unholy, an error of the Essenes, certain Gnostics, and (as Clarke applies it) later sects forbidding clergy to wed; and “commanding to abstain from meats” — imposing the avoidance of foods as if they defiled. The refutation: these God “created to be received with thanksgiving” — his good gifts, meant to be used with gratitude, not shunned as evil. The false teachers thus slander the Creator, calling unclean what he made good; Ellicott notes this would grow into full Gnostic dogma.
4:
On 1 Timothy 4:4 — “For every creature of God is good, and nothing to be refused, if it be received with thanksgiving” — the witnesses mark the goodness of creation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the principle. Robertson: “creature” (ktisma), the thing created (Genesis 1:31); “to be rejected” (apobleton). Adam Clarke: every creature made for nourishment is good, “to be thankfully received.” Ellicott: to teach that anything created is unclean insults the Creator.

The verse states the great principle against asceticism. Every creature of God is good, and nothing is to be refused if received with thanksgiving. Creation is good; gratitude hallows its use.

So the witnesses see Paul refute the false asceticism by the goodness of God’s creation. The witnesses note the broad principle: “every creature of God is good” — everything God made, as he made it, is good, echoing the verdict of Genesis that God saw all he had made and it was very good; the created order, including food and marriage, is not evil but good. They mark the inference: “nothing to be refused” — nothing God gave for use is to be rejected or despised as unclean; the ascetic taboos have no warrant. They observe the condition that hallows the use: “if it be received with thanksgiving” — the gifts are to be taken gratefully, as from God’s hand, with acknowledgment of him the Giver. They note Ellicott’s point that to teach any created thing unclean is an insult to the Creator, who pronounced it good. They observe that this strikes at the root of the error: it is not the use of God’s gifts but the contempt of them that offends God. The verse thus lays down the answer to false asceticism: all God’s creatures are good and may be used freely, when received with the thankfulness that honors the Giver — a use that, the next verse adds, is sanctified by the word of God and prayer.

So the witnesses see Paul refute the false asceticism by the goodness of God’s creation. The broad principle: “every creature of God is good” — everything he made, as he made it, echoing Genesis that God saw all was very good; food and marriage are not evil but good. The inference: “nothing to be refused” — nothing given for use is to be rejected as unclean. The condition that hallows the use: “if it be received with thanksgiving” — taken gratefully, as from God’s hand. To teach any created thing unclean is an insult to the Creator (Ellicott); it is contempt of the gifts, not their use, that offends God.
5:
On 1 Timothy 4:5 — “For it is sanctified by the word of God and prayer” — the witnesses mark the hallowing of God’s gifts.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the means. Robertson: “sanctified” (hagiazetai), rendered holy; “through the word of God and prayer” (dia logou theou kai enteuxeos) — a reference to Genesis 1, almost a hendiadys. Barnes: made holy for our use by God’s permission. Wesley: the children of God pray for the sanctification of all they use.

The verse explains how the gifts are hallowed. They are sanctified by the word of God and prayer. Scripture’s warrant and the believer’s prayer set them apart for holy use.

So the witnesses see Paul complete the answer to asceticism: God’s gifts are hallowed for use. The witnesses note that the creature, good in itself, is “sanctified” — set apart and rendered holy for the believer’s use — by two means. First, “the word of God” — the divine word that pronounced all things good and gave them to man for food (the witnesses note the reference to Genesis 1, where God created and granted them); Scripture’s warrant declares them clean and free for use. Second, “prayer” — the believer’s thanksgiving and petition over the gift, asking God’s blessing and acknowledging him the Giver; by grace at the table the common food is consecrated. They observe that the two together — the word that grants and the prayer that blesses — set the gift apart for holy use, so that what the ascetic calls unclean the believer receives as sanctified. They note Wesley’s point that the children of God pray for the sanctification of all they use, even as Jews and heathen consecrated their tables. They observe that this gives the positive practice against the false prohibitions: not abstinence, but grateful, prayerful use. The verse thus closes the refutation: God’s good gifts are made holy by his word and the believer’s prayer — received, not refused, and consecrated in the using.

So the witnesses see Paul complete the answer to asceticism: God’s gifts are hallowed for use. The creature, good in itself, is “sanctified” — set apart for use — by two means. First, “the word of God” — the divine word that pronounced all things good and gave them to man (ref. Genesis 1); Scripture’s warrant declares them clean. Second, “prayer” — the believer’s thanksgiving over the gift, grace at the table consecrating common food. The two together — the word that grants and the prayer that blesses — set the gift apart for holy use. Wesley’s point: the children of God pray for the sanctification of all they use. Not abstinence, but grateful, prayerful use.
6:
On 1 Timothy 4:6 — “If thou put the brethren in remembrance of these things, thou shalt be a good minister of Jesus Christ, nourished up in the words of faith and of good doctrine” — the witnesses mark the mark of a good minister.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the duty. Robertson: “put… in mind” (hypotithemenos), suggesting; “a good minister” (kalos diakonos); “nourished” (entrephomenos), feeding on the words of faith. The Puritan Henry: good ministers are diligent, not advancing new notions. Ellicott: reminding them of the danger of false asceticism.

The verse gives the way to be a good minister. By reminding the brethren of these truths, Timothy will be a good servant of Christ, nourished on the words of faith. Faithful teaching, fed by sound doctrine, marks the minister.

So the witnesses see Paul define the good minister by faithful instruction in sound doctrine. The witnesses note the duty: “if thou put the brethren in remembrance of these things” — reminding them of the truths just stated, especially the goodness of God’s gifts against the false asceticism; the minister’s task is not to invent novelties but to keep the people mindful of the truth. They mark the result: “thou shalt be a good minister of Jesus Christ” — faithful warning and teaching is the mark of a good servant of Christ; Henry notes that good ministers are diligent in this, not advancing new notions but establishing the brethren in the old truth. They observe the inward condition: “nourished up in the words of faith and of good doctrine” — the minister himself must be fed and built up on the sound words of the faith; he can only nourish others from the store on which he himself feeds. They note that this joins teaching and self-edification: the good minister feeds the flock from the doctrine that has fed his own soul. They observe the contrast with the false teachers, whose conscience was seared and whose doctrine was demonic. The verse thus sets the pattern of a good minister: one who reminds the brethren of the truth and is himself nourished on the words of faith — faithful in doctrine, against the fables Paul will now bid Timothy refuse.
7:
On 1 Timothy 4:7 — “But refuse profane and old wives’ fables, and exercise thyself rather unto godliness” — the witnesses mark the contrast of fables and godliness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Robertson: “refuse” (paraitou), beg off from; “old wives’ fables” (graodeis mythous), silly myths. Adam Clarke: the ridiculous fables of Jewish tradition or Romish legend. Wesley: “exercise thyself” like an athlete, “unto godliness… with the utmost labour.”

The verse sets fables against godliness. Refuse profane old wives’ tales, and train yourself rather toward godliness. Reject myth; pursue holiness.

So the witnesses see Paul direct Timothy away from foolish myths to the pursuit of godliness. The witnesses note the command to reject: “refuse profane and old wives’ fables” — have nothing to do with the silly, unholy myths and legends — whether the speculations of the false teachers, the fanciful traditions of Jewish lore, or, as Clarke extends it, the ridiculous legends of later superstition; such tales are “profane” (unhallowed, common) and fit for “old wives,” trifling and beneath a minister’s attention. They mark the positive command, drawn from the athletic arena: “exercise thyself rather unto godliness” — train, discipline, labor at the cultivation of true piety, as an athlete trains for the games, with effort, vigor, and diligence. They observe the contrast: instead of wasting time on empty myths, Timothy is to spend his strength on the pursuit of holiness of heart and life. They note Wesley’s point that this is to be done with the utmost labor, like the contestants in the Grecian games. They observe that the metaphor of exercise leads naturally into the comparison of bodily and spiritual training in the next verse. The verse thus turns Timothy from the false teachers’ fables to the strenuous cultivation of godliness — the one thing worth the labor, whose profit Paul now weighs against mere bodily exercise.

So the witnesses see Paul direct Timothy away from foolish myths to the pursuit of godliness. The command to reject: “refuse profane and old wives’ fables” — have nothing to do with the silly, unholy myths, whether the false teachers’ speculations, Jewish lore, or later superstition; such tales are profane and trifling, beneath a minister’s attention. The positive command, from the athletic arena: “exercise thyself rather unto godliness” — train, discipline, labour at the cultivation of true piety, as an athlete trains for the games, with effort and diligence (Wesley: with the utmost labour). Instead of empty myths, Timothy is to spend his strength on holiness.
8:
On 1 Timothy 4:8 — “For bodily exercise profiteth little: but godliness is profitable unto all things, having promise of the life that now is, and of that which is to come” — the witnesses mark the surpassing profit of godliness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the comparison. Robertson: “bodily exercise” (somatike gymnasia), “profitable for a little.” Adam Clarke: gymnastic exercises are of short duration; godliness has “the promise of this life and the life to come.” Ellicott: Paul thinks of the bodily austerities of v. 3. Wesley: bodily exercise “profiteth a little.”

The verse weighs bodily and spiritual exercise. Bodily training profits little; godliness profits in everything, holding promise for this life and the next. Godliness is the surpassing gain.

So the witnesses see Paul exalt godliness above all bodily discipline. The witnesses note the comparison’s lesser term: “bodily exercise profiteth little” — whether the athletic training of the games or, as Ellicott reads it, the bodily austerities of the false ascetics (fasting, abstinence, self-denial); such bodily discipline has some small or short-lived value but is limited, profitable “for a little.” They mark the surpassing greater term: “godliness is profitable unto all things” — true piety, the cultivation of holiness, is useful and beneficial in every respect, for body and soul, time and eternity. They observe the ground of its profit: “having promise of the life that now is, and of that which is to come” — godliness carries God’s promise of blessing both for the present life and for the life to come; it secures good in both worlds. They note Clarke’s contrast: bodily exercise is of short duration and earthly profit, but godliness has the double promise of this life and the next. They observe that this answers the false asceticism, which made bodily severity the height of religion: Paul shows that such bodily discipline is of little worth beside godliness, which alone is all-profitable. The verse thus weighs the two and crowns godliness: of little profit is bodily exercise, but godliness profits in all things, with promise for this life and the life everlasting.
9:
On 1 Timothy 4:9 — “This is a faithful saying and worthy of all acceptation” — the witnesses mark the seal upon the truth of godliness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the formula. Robertson: the same words as 1 Timothy 1:15, here pointing to the preceding statement. Ellicott: a striking formula calling attention to a great truth of broad currency in the early church. Adam Clarke: “the truth of this doctrine none need doubt.”

The verse stamps the foregoing with a solemn seal. This is a faithful saying, worthy of full acceptance. The truth about godliness is certain and to be embraced by all.

So the witnesses see Paul seal the great statement about godliness with his weighty formula of attestation. The witnesses note the phrase: “a faithful saying and worthy of all acceptation” — the same solemn formula used elsewhere to mark a truth of the first importance, trustworthy and deserving to be received without reservation by all. They mark that here, as Robertson observes, it points back to the preceding declaration — that godliness is profitable unto all things, with promise of this life and the next; that great truth is stamped as certain and to be embraced. They observe Ellicott’s point that this formula calls attention to a saying already of broad currency among the early churches, a recognized watchword of the faith. They note Clarke’s assurance that the truth of this doctrine need not be doubted, and that every man may put it to the proof. They observe that the solemn seal both confirms the worth of godliness and prepares the reason Paul gives for it in the next verse — the labor and reproach the apostles bear because they trust the living God. The verse thus underlines the certainty of the foregoing: the surpassing profit of godliness is a faithful saying, worthy of full acceptance — a truth so sure that for it Paul and his fellows gladly labor and suffer.

So the witnesses see Paul seal the great statement about godliness with his weighty formula of attestation. The phrase “a faithful saying and worthy of all acceptation” — the same solemn formula marking a truth of the first importance, trustworthy and to be received without reservation. Here, as Robertson observes, it points back to the preceding declaration — that godliness is profitable unto all things, with promise of this life and the next. Ellicott’s point: a saying already of broad currency among the early churches, a recognized watchword. The solemn seal confirms the worth of godliness and prepares the reason Paul gives in the next verse.
10:
On 1 Timothy 4:10 — “For therefore we both labour and suffer reproach, because we trust in the living God, who is the Saviour of all men, specially of those that believe” — the witnesses mark the ground of apostolic toil.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the hope. Robertson: “we labour and strive” (kopiomen kai agonizometha); “set our hope” (elpikamen) on the living God. Wesley: God “the Saviour of all men” in preserving them, “specially of those that believe” in the higher, saving sense. Gill: Preserver of all, Saviour especially of believers.

The verse gives the reason for the apostles’ toil. For godliness’ sake they labor and bear reproach, trusting the living God, Saviour of all, especially of believers. Hope in God sustains their suffering.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the apostles’ labor and suffering in their hope in the living God. The witnesses note the motive: “therefore” — for the sake of godliness and its promise — “we both labour and suffer reproach”; the apostles toil hard and endure scorn precisely because they pursue the godliness that profits unto all things and looks for the life to come. They mark the spring of their endurance: “because we trust in the living God” — their hope is fixed on the living God, who is able to make good his promise of life. They observe the much-discussed clause: God “is the Saviour of all men, specially of those that believe.” The witnesses note the expositors’ reading: this does not teach universal salvation, but that God is the Preserver and benefactor of all men, who gives life and good to all his creatures, and the Saviour in the full, redeeming sense “specially of those that believe” — the saving salvation belonging peculiarly to believers. They observe Wesley’s and Gill’s sense: Saviour of all in the providential preserving of all, but especially of believers in the eternal, saving sense. They note that this confidence in the living God, the giver of life and salvation, is what sustains the apostles through toil and reproach. The verse thus shows the reason of their endurance: hope in the living God, Saviour of all and especially of believers, makes the labor and shame of godliness worthwhile.
11:
On 1 Timothy 4:11 — “These things command and teach” — the witnesses mark the charge to enforce and instruct.

The Eastern fathers note the balance. Chrysostom: in some cases command, in others teach; to command where teaching is needed, or teach where commanding is needed, is to err. The grammatical-historical Barnes: a minister is bound to teach these doctrines. Adam Clarke: let this be the sum and substance of thy preaching. The Puritan Henry: those who teach by doctrine must teach by life.

The verse charges Timothy with his task. Command and teach these things. The minister is to enforce and instruct in the truths just stated.

So the witnesses see Paul charge Timothy to make these truths the matter of his ministry. The witnesses note the twofold duty: “command” — enjoin with authority, enforce as binding; and “teach” — instruct, explain, make plain. They mark Chrysostom’s wise observation on the balance: some matters require command (where the duty is clear and only obedience is needed), others require teaching (where understanding must be imparted); to command where one should teach, or teach where one should command, is to misjudge the case. They observe that “these things” are the truths just set forth — the goodness of God’s gifts, the surpassing profit of godliness, the hope in the living God; these are to be the substance of Timothy’s preaching, as Clarke says, the sum and substance of his message. They note Henry’s point that the minister must teach not only by doctrine but by life. They observe that this brief charge gathers up the foregoing and turns it into Timothy’s standing commission: to press these truths upon the people with both authority and instruction. The verse thus directs the minister’s task: command and teach the truths of godliness — and prepares the personal exhortation to Timothy that his own youth and example may give weight to that teaching.

So the witnesses see Paul charge Timothy to make these truths the matter of his ministry. The twofold duty: “command” — enjoin with authority, enforce as binding; and “teach” — instruct, explain, make plain. Chrysostom’s wise observation on the balance: some matters require command (where the duty is clear), others require teaching (where understanding must be imparted); to mistake the case is to err. “These things” are the truths just set forth — the goodness of God’s gifts, the profit of godliness, hope in the living God — the substance of Timothy’s preaching. Henry’s point: the minister must teach by life as well as doctrine.
12:
On 1 Timothy 4:12 — “Let no man despise thy youth; but be thou an example of the believers, in word, in conversation, in charity, in spirit, in faith, in purity” — the witnesses mark the call to be an example.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the charge. Robertson: “despise” (kataphroneito), think down on; “be thou” (ginou), keep on becoming. Adam Clarke: act with the gravity that becomes the office; “boyish playfulness ill becomes a minister.” Wesley: be a pattern “in word… in spirit… in faith.”

The verse meets the obstacle of youth. Let no one despise Timothy’s youth; instead, let him be an example to believers in every grace. A holy example overcomes the disadvantage of youth.

So the witnesses see Paul counsel the youthful Timothy to overcome contempt by exemplary character. The witnesses note the danger: “let no man despise thy youth” — Timothy, being comparatively young for so weighty a charge, might be looked down upon; he is to give no occasion for it. They mark the remedy: “be thou an example of the believers” — not to demand respect, but to earn it by being a pattern, a model the believers themselves may copy; the way to disarm contempt is a life worthy of imitation. They observe the spheres of the example, covering the whole man: “in word” (his speech, public and private), “in conversation” (his whole manner of life and conduct), “in charity” (love), “in spirit” (his temper and disposition), “in faith” (fidelity and trust), and “in purity” (chastity and holiness). They note Clarke’s point that the young minister must act with the gravity his office requires, for boyish levity ill becomes a minister of the gospel. They observe that the present tense — “keep on becoming” — marks this as a continual pursuit. The verse thus shows how youth is to be overcome: not by self-assertion but by a holy, exemplary life in word, conduct, love, spirit, faith, and purity — a pattern that earns the respect Timothy’s years might not command.

So the witnesses see Paul counsel the youthful Timothy to overcome contempt by exemplary character. The danger: “let no man despise thy youth” — Timothy, young for so weighty a charge, must give no occasion for it. The remedy: “be thou an example of the believers” — not to demand respect but to earn it by being a pattern to copy. The spheres cover the whole man: “word” (speech), “conversation” (conduct), “charity” (love), “spirit” (temper), “faith” (fidelity), and “purity.” Clarke’s point: the young minister must act with the gravity his office requires, for boyish levity ill becomes a minister. Youth is overcome not by self-assertion but by a holy life.
13:
On 1 Timothy 4:13 — “Till I come, give attendance to reading, to exhortation, to doctrine” — the witnesses mark the threefold ministerial duty.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the tasks. Robertson: “give heed” (proseche), keep putting the mind on; “the reading” (anagnosei), probably public reading of Scripture. Adam Clarke: the books of the Old Testament. Ellicott: three special points to attend to. Wesley: “give thyself to reading… expect no end without the means.”

The verse names Timothy’s standing duties. Until Paul comes, he is to attend to reading, exhortation, and teaching. Public ministry rests on these three.

So the witnesses see Paul direct Timothy to the central exercises of public ministry. The witnesses note that these are to occupy him “till I come” — the regular duties to be diligently pursued in Paul’s absence. They mark the three: “reading” — most take this as the public reading of the Scriptures in the assembly (the Old Testament chiefly, as Clarke notes, though also private study to feed the ministry); “exhortation” — the application of the word to the conscience, urging and encouraging the hearers to duty; and “doctrine” — the teaching of sound truth, instructing the people in the faith. They observe that these three together make up the staple of the minister’s public work: the word read, applied, and taught. They note Wesley’s point that Timothy is to give himself to reading both publicly and privately, and his pointed remark to enthusiasts — “expect no end without the means”; the minister must use the appointed means of study and teaching, not look for fruit apart from diligent labor. They observe that this is the practical heart of Timothy’s charge: faithful attention to the public ministry of the word. The verse thus sets the minister’s standing tasks: reading, exhortation, and doctrine — to which Timothy is to give himself wholly, with the gift God has bestowed, as the next verses urge.

So the witnesses see Paul direct Timothy to the central exercises of public ministry. These are to occupy him “till I come” — the regular duties pursued in Paul’s absence. The three: “reading” — the public reading of Scripture in the assembly (the OT chiefly, Clarke, and private study); “exhortation” — the application of the word to the conscience, urging to duty; and “doctrine” — the teaching of sound truth. Together these make up the staple of the minister’s public work: the word read, applied, and taught. Wesley’s pointed remark to enthusiasts: expect no end without the means — the minister must use the appointed means, not look for fruit apart from diligent labour.
14:
On 1 Timothy 4:14 — “Neglect not the gift that is in thee, which was given thee by prophecy, with the laying on of the hands of the presbytery” — the witnesses mark the charge to use the ministerial gift.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the gift and its conferral. Robertson: “neglect not” (me amelei); “the gift” (charismatos), the spiritual endowment (2 Timothy 1:6). Adam Clarke: the gifts and graces of the Spirit received at his ordination. Wesley: the gift of feeding the flock, “given… by prophecy… the laying on of my hands… the elders joining.”

The verse charges Timothy to use his gift. He is not to neglect the spiritual gift given him by prophecy and the laying on of the elders’ hands. The ordained gift is to be exercised, not let lie idle.

So the witnesses see Paul charge Timothy to stir up and use the gift bestowed at his ordination. The witnesses note the warning: “neglect not the gift that is in thee” — the spiritual endowment for ministry is not to be let lie unused; to neglect it, Wesley notes, is to fail to exercise it to the full. They mark its nature: a “gift” (charisma), the grace and ability for the work — for feeding the flock, ruling, teaching — which is God’s bestowal, not natural talent or human learning. They observe the manner of its conferral: “given thee by prophecy” — pointed out or accompanied by prophetic direction marking Timothy for the office; “with the laying on of the hands of the presbytery” — at his ordination, when the elders (and Paul himself, as 2 Timothy adds) laid their hands on him, the solemn setting-apart to the ministry. They note the harmony with the parallel passage, where Paul speaks of “my hands”: Paul and the elders together ordained him. They observe that the charge is to active use: the gift, once received, must be employed, cultivated, and not allowed to grow cold. The verse thus presses the duty of diligence: Timothy is to use the ministerial gift given at his ordination — a charge Paul deepens in the next verses, bidding him give himself wholly to these things.
15:
On 1 Timothy 4:15 — “Meditate upon these things; give thyself wholly to them; that thy profiting may appear to all” — the witnesses mark the call to wholehearted devotion.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the diligence. Robertson: “be diligent in these things” (tauta meleta), keep on practising; “give thyself wholly to them” (en toutois isthi), be in these things. Adam Clarke: revolve them frequently in thy mind. Wesley: “he that is wholly in these will be little in worldly things.”

The verse calls for total devotion. Meditate on these things and be wholly absorbed in them, that progress may be evident to all. Wholehearted application brings visible growth.

So the witnesses see Paul call Timothy to wholehearted devotion to his ministry. The witnesses note the first command: “meditate upon these things” — or, be diligent in them; let his mind dwell on, revolve, and practise the truths and duties just named, pondering them deeply until they shape his whole conduct. They mark the second, stronger command: “give thyself wholly to them” — literally, be in these things, be absorbed and immersed in them, making the ministry the one great business of his life. They observe Wesley’s pointed comment: he who is wholly in these things will be little in worldly affairs; total devotion to the ministry leaves little room for entanglement in lesser concerns. They note the aim: “that thy profiting may appear to all” — that his progress, his growth in grace and gifts, may be evident and manifest to everyone; diligent, wholehearted application produces visible advancement that commends the minister and his message. They observe that this answers the matter of his youth: as he visibly grows, contempt gives way to respect. The verse thus presses entire devotion: Timothy is to meditate on and be wholly given to his ministry, that his evident progress may be seen by all — a self-watch that the climactic next verse extends to his soul and his hearers.

So the witnesses see Paul call Timothy to wholehearted devotion to his ministry. The first command: “meditate upon these things” — be diligent in them; let his mind dwell on, revolve, and practise the truths and duties named, until they shape his conduct. The second, stronger: “give thyself wholly to them” — literally, be in these things, absorbed and immersed, making the ministry the one great business of his life. Wesley’s comment: he who is wholly in these will be little in worldly affairs. The aim: “that thy profiting may appear to all” — that his progress be evident; diligent application produces visible growth that answers the matter of his youth.
16:
On 1 Timothy 4:16 — “Take heed unto thyself, and unto the doctrine; continue in them: for in doing this thou shalt both save thyself, and them that hear thee” — the witnesses mark the climactic charge.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the double watch. Robertson: “take heed to thyself” (epeche seauto), keep paying attention to thyself. Adam Clarke: heed thy own soul and thy doctrine, that it be pure and orthodox. Ellicott: the two chief pastoral requisites. Wesley: “continue in them — in all the preceding advices.”

The verse gives the crowning charge. Watch yourself and your teaching; persevere in them, and you will save both yourself and your hearers. Self and doctrine are the two cares, perseverance the duty, salvation the fruit.

So the witnesses see Paul close the charge with a weighty, comprehensive command. The witnesses note the double object of watchfulness: “take heed unto thyself” — to his own soul, character, and life, that the inward grace be kept and the personal walk be holy; and “unto the doctrine” — to his teaching, that it be pure, sound, and faithful. They mark Ellicott’s point that these are the two chief pastoral requisites: a holy life and sound teaching; the minister must guard both, for a flaw in either ruins his ministry. They observe the duty of perseverance: “continue in them” — persist steadfastly in this twofold heed, in all the foregoing counsels, not for a season but to the end. They note the great promise that crowns it: “thou shalt both save thyself, and them that hear thee” — not that the minister is the author of salvation, but that, under God, faithful attention to his own soul and his doctrine is the appointed means by which he secures his own salvation and becomes the instrument of his hearers’. They observe the solemn weight: the minister’s faithfulness bears on the eternal welfare both of himself and of those he teaches. The verse thus seals the chapter with the highest motive: watch self and doctrine, persevere, and be the means, under God, of saving both yourself and your hearers — the crown of the charge to the man of God.
